coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly of MOFs-like materials by Zn2+ and histidine functionalized graphene quantum dots for the efficient removal of VOCs simulants
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Youliang
Zhang, Yue
Li, Jiaxian
Fang, Changqing
Chen, Jing
Zhao, Jiarui
Zhao, Tong
Zhu, Jia
coredata.dc:description: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the field of printing and packaging, have caused great harm to the environment. Therefore, the removal of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) is a hot topic, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been applied in this field. However, improving the removal efficiency of different VOCs by using the two kinds of materials is still a great challenge. Herein, ZIF-8 like materials of Zn-His-GQDs were prepared by assembling Zn2+ and histidine functionalized GQDs (His-GQDs) in this work. The morphologies, structure and adsorption performances of Zn-His-GQDs were investigated. These results showed that mesoporous Zn-His-GQDs with a specific surface area of 63.3 m2·g−1 had similar structure to ZIF-8. When the content of Zn-His-GQDs was 200 mg, the equilibrium adsorption capacity for toluene solution of 500 mg·L−1 and ethyl acetate solution of 1600 mg·L−1 was 350 mg·L−1 and 669.44 mg·L−1, respectively. Due to channel filling, cation-π interaction, π-π interaction, hydrogen bond and high surface energy, Zn-His-GQDs exhibited a relative high removal efficiency for high concentration of toluene and ethyl acetate. The removal rate of Zn-His-GQDs on toluene and ethyl acetate after five cycles remained 59.80 % and 36.75 %, respectively. Moreover, this kind of MOFs like materials maintained stable structure during the adsorption in different pH solutions. Therefore, as-prepared Zn-His-GQDs with excellent adsorption performances for typical VOCs simulants in this work have potential application in the treatment of VOCs in the field of printing and packaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Zeolitic imidazolate framework
Mesoporous materials
Volatile organic compounds
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MOFs-like materials containing His-GQDs
Determination of the absorbance for toluene and ethyl acetate aqueous solution
Adsorption experiments of toluene and ethyl acetate
Morphologies and structures of as-prepared His-GQDs and Zn-His-GQDs
Chemical components and pore structure of Zn-His-GQDs
Adsorption performances of Zn-His-GQDs by using toluene and ethyl acetate as VOCs simulants
Adsorption thermodynamics of Zn-His-GQDs on toluene and ethyl acetate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFD1600402 ), Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2020GXLH-Z-031 ), Key Scientific Research Project of Weinan City (Grant Nos. 2020ZDYF-GYCX-107 and ZDYFJH-176 ), Key Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 20JS106 ), Beilin District Science and Technology Plan Project (Grant No. GX2248 ), Qindu District Science and Technology Plan Project (Grant No. QK2022gy11 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of His-GQDs.
FTIR spectrum (a), XRD pattern (b) and schematic structure (c) of Zn-His-GQDs.
TEM image of His-GQDs (a) and SEM image of Zn-His-GQDs (b).
XPS full spectrum (a), C 1s spectrum (b), N 1s spectrum (c) and Zn 2p spectrum (d) of Zn-His-GQDs.
EDS spectrum and mapping of Zn-His-GQDs.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (a) and pore diameter distribution (b) of Zn-His-GQDs.
TG curve (a) and DTG curve (b) of Zn-His-GQDs.
The standard curves of absorbance vs concentration for TOL solution (a) and EtOAc solution (b) with different concentrations.
Effect of contact time (a) and pH with the contact time of 420 min (b) on adsorption properties of Zn-His-GQDs.
Possible adsorption mechanism illustration of Zn-His-GQDs to TOL.

Pseudo-first-order kinetic model (a) and pseudo-second-order kinetic model (b) for adsorption of Zn-His-GQDs to TOL, pseudo-first-order kinetic model (c) and pseudo-second-order kinetic model (d) for adsorption of Zn-His-GQDs to EtOAc.
Thermodynamic fitting curves for the adsorption process of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene and ethyl acetate.
Specific surface area and pore structure parameter of Zn-His-GQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: SBET is the specific surface area of the material, Smic is the micropore specific surface area of the material, Vtot is the total pore volume of the material, Vmic is the micropore pore volume of the material.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorbance values for TOL solution with different concentrations.
Absorbance values for EtOAc solution with different concentrations.
Adsorption kinetics parameters of Zn-His-GQDs to TOL.
Thermodynamic parameters of toluene adsorption by Zn-His-GQDs.
Thermodynamic parameters of ethyl acetate adsorption by Zn-His-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly of MOFs-like materials by Zn2+ and histidine functionalized graphene quantum dots for the efficient removal of VOCs simulants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Printing, Packaging Engineering and Digital Media Technology, Xi'’an University of Technology, Xi'’an 710048, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering, Xi'’an University of Technology, Xi'’an 710048, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the field of printing and packaging, have caused great harm to the environment. Therefore, the removal of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) is a hot topic, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been applied in this field. However, improving the removal efficiency of different VOCs by using the two kinds of materials is still a great challenge. Herein, ZIF-8 like materials of Zn-His-GQDs were prepared by assembling Zn2+ and histidine functionalized GQDs (His-GQDs) in this work. The morphologies, structure and adsorption performances of Zn-His-GQDs were investigated. These results showed that mesoporous Zn-His-GQDs with a specific surface area of 63.3 m2·g−1 had similar structure to ZIF-8. When the content of Zn-His-GQDs was 200 mg, the equilibrium adsorption capacity for toluene solution of 500 mg·L−1 and ethyl acetate solution of 1600 mg·L−1 was 350 mg·L−1 and 669.44 mg·L−1, respectively. Due to channel filling, cation-π interaction, π-π interaction, hydrogen bond and high surface energy, Zn-His-GQDs exhibited a relative high removal efficiency for high concentration of toluene and ethyl acetate. The removal rate of Zn-His-GQDs on toluene and ethyl acetate after five cycles remained 59.80 % and 36.75 %, respectively. Moreover, this kind of MOFs like materials maintained stable structure during the adsorption in different pH solutions. Therefore, as-prepared Zn-His-GQDs with excellent adsorption performances for typical VOCs simulants in this work have potential application in the treatment of VOCs in the field of printing and packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) generated in the field of printing and packaging still cause great harm to the environment and human health [1,2]. The detection, monitoring and removal of VOCs remains a hot topic. Therefore, numerous methods such as adsorption, combustion and other chemical methods have been proposed for addressing this issue. In particular, adsorption technology was favored and admired due to the lack of by-products, high adsorption efficiency, flexible and simple operation [3]. Currently, activated carbons, molecular sieves and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been widely used to remove VOCs by the adsorption [4,5]. However, a relatively low adsorption capacity, inactivation of adsorption sites and poor selectivity are innegligible issues for these materials. Thus, a novel method by combining the adsorption and catalysis is proposed, and it will be more efficient to remove VOCs [6].

To our knowledge, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess excellent luminescence properties [7], low toxicity [8] and good biocompatibility [9,10], which have been applied in photovoltaic cells [11], biological imaging and medical fields [12]. Meanwhile, GQDs also attract much attention of researchers in the field of adsorption-catalysis due to the large specific surface area, abundant active sites, and tunable properties [13]. However, normal GQDs without modification may be limited in some special fields. Fortunately, functionalized GQDs after suitable design and/or modification can meet these requirements [11]. Qu and co-workers prepared nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQD) via hydrothermal method by using citric acid and urea as raw materials [14], which exhibited a tunable light-emitting performance. Wang and co-workers prepared N-GQDs modified nanoporous carbon nitride (N-GQD@npg-C3N4) by one-pot co-sintering method [15], which exhibited high photocatalytic activity for degrading organic dyes under solar radiation. Nitrogen atom has a low dielectric constant and a wide energy band gap [16], and this kind of strong electron donor is easily connected to GQDs through doping and chemical modification. Then, the energy band and electronic properties for GQDs based materials are also changed, which may improve the adsorption capacity for VOCs.

According to previous report [17], zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) have a similar topology to that for zeolite molecular sieves and MOFs. Additionally, ZIFs have the advantages of good thermal stability, tunable structure and ultra-high specific surface area, which have potential applications in gas storage and separation [18,19], adsorption [20,21], photocatalysis [22,23] and sensors [24]. As a typical representative in the ZIFs series, ZIF-8 materials have unique properties compared with traditional inorganic materials, including many basic sites provided by -OH and N, L acid sites provided by Zn2+, and B acid sites provided by NH. These abundantly active sites for ZIF-8 can adsorb different pollutants [25]. Therefore, these characteristics of GQDs and ZIF-8 provide the possibility of efficiently removing VOCs pollutants. However, it remains a great challenge to use the synergy of the two materials to achieve more efficient adsorption of VOCs. In our previous report [26], GQDs@ZIF-8 composites were synthesized by directly incorporating GQDs with ZIF-8, which exhibiting high adsorption capacity for VOCs simulants. Nevertheless, the combination between GQDs and ZIF-8 in this kind of composite may be weak. Thus, it is necessary to develop similar materials via a new route for fully taking advantages of GQDs and ZIF-8 materials.

Obviously, there are numerous active sites after the functionalization of GQDs and Zn2+ can be grafted to functionalized GQDs [27]. Herein, GQDs were firstly functionalized with histidine, and then Zn2+ ions were introduced, which enabled functionalized GQDs and Zn2+ to form nanoparticles with MOFs-like structure through ligand bonding. This strategy can endow this kind of MOFs-like materials with excellent performances on the removal of VOCs. Additionally, the morphologies, microstructures and adsorption performances of as-prepared MOFs-like materials were investigated. Results revealed that this kind material exhibited the equilibrium adsorption capacity of 350 mg·L−1 for toluene and the equilibrium adsorption capacity of 669.44 mg·L−1 for ethyl acetate under set experimental conditions. Therefore, this kind of MOFs-like materials can be used to efficiently adsorb typical VOCs in the field of printing and packaging.

Citric acid was purchased from Tianjin Jiayu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (37 wt%) was purchased from Luoyang Haohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Zinc chloride and histidine were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used for through experimental process.

Typically, 1 g of citric acid and 0.74 g of histidine was mixed in deionized water with stirring until the mixture was colorless, and then it was dried for 24 h in vacuum freezing drying oven. Subsequently, obtained white solid was heated at 220 °C, and it gradually turned into an orange-yellow liquid during the heating treatment. 0.25 mol·L−1 NaOH solution was added dropwise to above orange-yellow liquid with magnetically stirring at 500 rpm for 15 min. The mass ratio of NaOH solution to the orange-yellow liquid was 30:1. The pH of above cooling mixture was adjusted to 7 by using 10 wt% dilute hydrochloric acid, and the solution obtained was dialyzed in a 7000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h then transferred to a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The deionized water was replaced every 6 h during dialyzing. Finally, collected inner fluid was dried to obtain histidine functionalized GQDs solid denoted as His-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MOFs-like materials containing His-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.05 mol·L−1 ZnCl2 solution and 2.0 mg·mL−1 His-GQDs solution was prepared, respectively. The volume ratio of ZnCl2 solution to His-GQDs solution was set to 1:5, and the two solutions were blended with stirring at 700 rpm for 15 min before stirring at 300 rpm for 5 min. Finally, the products were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and then washed with deionized water for three times. After dried for 12 h at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven, MOFs-like materials containing His-GQDs were obtained and they were denoted as Zn-His-GQDs.

FTIR spectra of samples were obtained by a FTIR spectrometer (Shimadzu IRSpirt) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. XRD patterns were measured by an X-ray diffractometer (SHIMADZU LIMITED XRD-7000) in the range of 5–50°. Surface morphologies of samples were observed by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi SU8000) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). Components of samples were determined by using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) and above FESEM with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). UV–vis spectra were collected by a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-1800). Thermalgravimeric analysis was investigated by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, NETZSCH TG 209F3) in the range of 25–800 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1 under N2 atmosphere. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured on a specific surface analyzer (Quanta chrome NOVA) at 77 K, and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model were employed to analyze the specific surface area and pore structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the absorbance for toluene and ethyl acetate aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: To our knowledge, when the temperature is 293 K, the solubility of toluene in water is 0.05 g mL−1, and that of ethyl acetate in water is 8.3 g 100 mL−1. Different concentrations of toluene aqueous solution and ethyl acetate aqueous solution were prepared at 293 K. Then the absorbance of toluene solution at 261 nm and ethyl acetate solution at 256 nm were measured using Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption experiments of toluene and ethyl acetate
ce:section.ce:para:
Toluene and ethyl acetate was used as VOCs simulants to investigate the adsorption performances of Zn-His-GQDs. 200 mL of toluene solution (500 mg·L−1) and ethyl acetate solution (1600 mg·L−1) was put into the conical flasks, respectively. Then, 200 mg of Zn-His-GQDs was added to the conical flasks with magnetic stirring of 300 rpm at 293 K for different time during adsorption experiments. Subsequently, the adsorbed solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min for isolating the adsorbent, and the supernatant was collected. Finally, the absorbance of the supernatant containing toluene was measured at 261 nm and the absorbance of the supernatant containing ethyl acetate was measured at 256 nm using Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The absorbance of standard solutions of toluene and ethyl acetate was used to calculate the concentration of toluene and ethyl acetate in the supernatants. The removal rate and adsorption capacity of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene and ethyl acetate can be calculated according to Eqs. (1) and (2) [28–30]:
(1)
η =
C i
−
C t
C i
× 100 %
where Ci was initial concentration of adsorbate (mg·L−1), Ct was the concentration of the adsorbate in the supernatant at contact time t (mg·L−1), and η was removal rate of adsorbate (%).
(2)
q t
=
C i
−
C t
× V
m
where V was the volume of adsorbate solution during the experiment (L),
m
was dosing mass of adsorbent during the experiment (g), qt was adsorption capacity of the adsorbent (mg·g−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and structures of as-prepared His-GQDs and Zn-His-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1(a), XRD pattern of His-GQDs had an obvious broad diffraction peak at about 2θ = 25°, indicating that the arrangement of carbon layers in His-GQDs was disordered graphite-like [31]. In order to confirm that the imidazole group has been successfully grafted on GQDs, FTIR spectra of His-GQDs, histidine and citric acid were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1(b), for FTIR spectrum of His-GQDs, the characteristic absorption peaks belonging to citric acid and histidine basically disappeared, and the broad peak around 3410 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration peaks of -OH and –NH [32]. In addition, adsorption peaks different from citric acid and histidine also appeared in His-GQDs, and the vibrational peaks located at 1700 cm−1, 1595 cm−1, 1393 cm−1, 1205 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 were assigned to CO, CC, CN, =C-O-, CO stretching vibration [32], respectively. These results show that the surface of His-GQDs contains a large number of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), the adsorption peak at 1575 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CN, and the absorption band in the range of 1350–1500 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of the imidazole ring. These adsorption peaks in FTIR spectrum of Zn-His-GQD proved the characteristic groups of imidazole unit. In addition, the adsorption peak around 3500 cm−1 should correspond to the stretching vibration peak of OH for as-prepared GQDs. Compared with FTIR spectrum of His-GQDs, adsorption peaks for same groups in FTIR spectrum of Zn-His-GQDs shifted a little due to the change of chemical environment. Besides normal adsorption peaks, the abnormal adsorption peak at 510 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CS due to the impurity of raw materials or the contact with other reagents containing S during experimental operation. As shown in Fig. 2(b), there was a weak diffraction peak around 25°corresponding to (002) crystal plane of graphite carbons for GQDs. In addition, the diffraction peaks around 10° and 18° corresponded to (002) and (222) crystal planes of ZIF-8, respectively [33]. These results also verify the MOFs-like structure for Zn-His-GQDs and the existence of His-GQDs in this framework (in Fig. 2(c)), while other XRD characteristic peaks of ZIF-8 type MOFs are difficult to observe due to the low crystallinity.

As shown in Fig. 3(a), as-prepared spherical GQDs were uniform with no aggregations, which of size was in the range of 1–3 nm. For SEM image of Zn-His-GQDs (in Fig. 3(b)), it can be seen that the size of these polygonal nanoparticles was in the range of 20–40 nm, which of morphology was similar to ZIF-8 in previous reports due to the combination of Zn2+ and imidazole groups [34]. The difference in the morphology of ZIF-8 may be mainly attributed to the introduction of His-GQDs. Compared with one-dimensional 2-methylimidazole, nanoparticles of His-GQDs with a large number of edge groups had larger size and higher steric hindrance, which greatly restricted the growth and alignment of crystals [35]. Therefore, ZIF-8 like Zn-His-GQDs showed tiny and irregular nanocrystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical components and pore structure of Zn-His-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4(a), the XPS full spectrum of Zn-His-GQDs confirmed the presence of C, N and Zn elements. For the C 1s spectrum (in Fig. 4(b)), there were four peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.74 eV, 287.81 eV and 291.67 eV, which were assigned to CC, CN, CO and CS, respectively. According to this result, the introduction of trace impurity element of S was consistent with the analysis of FTIR results. Additionally, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (in Fig. 4(c)) revealed three peaks at 399.16 eV, 399.53 eV and 401.68 eV, corresponding to NC, NH and NZn, respectively. To our knowledge, the peaks at 285.74 eV, 399.16 eV and 399.53 eV corresponded to graphitic-N, pyridinic-N and amine-N [36,37]. Furthermore, the Zn 2p spectrum (in Fig. 4(d)) exhibited two peaks at 1022.18 eV and 1045.25 eV, corresponding to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. The CN, CN and NZn were similar to that for ZIF-8 [38], which also confirmed the MOFs-like structure for Zn-His-GQDs. There were obvious C, N, O and Zn signals in EDS spectrum of Zn-His-GQDs (in Fig. 5(a)), and the distribution of these elements was basically consistent with their position in the frame (in Fig.5(b)-(g)), supporting the analysis of morphology and XPS for Zn-His-GQDs.

Fig. 6(a) displays the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of Zn-His-GQDs. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification [39,40], the N2 adsorption isotherm of Zn-His-GQDs belonged to type IV. In addition, there was a significant H3 hysteresis loop during the process of the N2 adsorption-desorption, which was caused by the mesopore adsorption for Zn-His-GQDs composites. At the region of low relative pressure (P/P0 < 0.1), N2 molecules rapidly entered the pore channels of Zn-His-GQDs due to micropore adsorption, resulting in a significant increase of adsorption amount for N2. Subsequently, as the relative pressure increased, N2 molecules filled all the pore channels of Zn-His-GQDs, and then a multilayer adsorption gradually was formed, resulting in the continuous increase of adsorption amount for N2. When P/P0 was close to 1, the adsorption amount rose dramatically due to the adsorption on the outer surface, and the adsorption curve showed an asymptote close to the vertical axis [41]. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the diameter for the majority of pores in Zn-His-GQDs was in the range of 2–30 nm. According to Table 1 , SBET of Zn-His-GQDs was 63.3 m2·g−1, which was lower than that for ZIF-8 materials [42]. This result was due to the special crystal structure and porous structure formed by the assembly of His-GQDs and Zn2+, and it was consistent with the analysis of XRD and SEM results. In addition, the Vtot was 0.483 cm3·g−1 and Vmic was only 0.02 cm3·g−1, which also confirmed dominant mesoporous structure for Zn-His-GQDs.

As shown in Fig. 7(a), the weight loss of Zn-His-GQDs was about 5.5 wt% due to the removal of water and other volatile substances before 200 °C. In the range of 200–400 °C, the weight loss of Zn-His-GQDs was about 11 wt% due to the removal of hydroxy and carboxyl groups [43]. After about 400 °C, the weight loss of Zn-His-GQDs increased rapidly due to the damage of MOFs-like skeleton structure of Zn-His-GQDs. According to DTG curve of Zn-His-GQDs (in Fig. 7(b)), the three weight loss peaks at about 95 °C, 250 °C and 400 °C due to H2O volatilization, oxygen-containing functional groups removal and framework damage also confirmed the deduction of the result of TG. According to only weight loss of 5.5 wt%, we inferred that as-prepared Zn-His-GQDs had relatively high thermal stability and it as the absorbent can be applied in most environment temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption performances of Zn-His-GQDs by using toluene and ethyl acetate as VOCs simulants
ce:section.ce:para:
Measured absorbance of toluene (TOL) solution with different concentrations at 261 nm and ethyl acetate (EtOAc) solution with different concentrations at 256 nm is shown in Tables 2 and 3
, respectively. The curves of absorbance vs concentration for toluene and ethyl acetate solutions are shown in Fig. 8 . According to the linear fitting, R2 for TOL and EtOAc reached 0.965 and 0.975, respectively, indicating that linear correlation of the two curves was relatively high.

The effects of contact time and pH on the adsorption capacity of Zn-His-GQDs were investigated by UV–vis spectra. Without adjusting pH, the adsorption capacity of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene and ethyl acetate increased rapidly within 60 min of contact time (in Fig. 9(a)), and that at real time of 60 min was 193.1 mg·g−1 and 525.9 mg·g−1, respectively. After contact time of 60 min, the adsorption rate decreased obviously. After 420 min, the adsorption capacity of toluene and ethyl acetate tended to be stable, and the equilibrium absorption capacity was 350 mg·g−1 and 669.44 mg·g−1, respectively. In addition, it can be calculated according the equilibrium adsorption capacity under experimental conditions that the removal rate of Zn-His-GQDs for toluene and ethyl acetate was 70 % and 41.84 %, respectively. The removal rate was relative low due to the high concentration of VOCs simulants in this work. To our knowledge, different pH of the system would affect the surface charge of the adsorbent and the separation of functional groups on the active sites in this system, which consequently have important influences on the adsorption behaviors of the adsorbent [44]. In this work, we investigated the effect of pH for toluene and ethyl acetate solutions on the adsorption performance of Zn-His-GQDs, and the results are displayed in Fig. 9(b). Notably, both of acidic and basic conditions can improve the removal rate of toluene and ethyl acetate, which is mainly due to the protonation and deprotonation of active sites at the edge of His-GQDs. As-prepared His-GQDs contained amino groups, carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups, and these active sites were favorable to the adsorption of toluene and ethyl acetate depending on the pH of the solution [45].

Toluene was used as the adsorbate example to analyze the adsorption mechanism of toluene by Zn-His-GQD. Fig. 10 shows the possible adsorption mechanism of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene molecules. It is mentioned that the Zn-His-GQDs composites prepared in this work are mesoporous. The adsorption of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene mainly consisted of three parts: mesoporous channel filling, cation-π interaction and π-π interaction. When toluene molecules contacted with Zn-His-GQDs, numerous functional groups on the surface of His-GQDs provided abundant active sites and then these toluene molecules easily diffused on the surface of Zn-His-GQDs. Subsequently, adsorbed toluene molecules gradually diffused into the mesopore channels of Zn-His-GQDs and finally deposited in the pore channels to complete mesoporous channel filling. Furthermore, the cation-π interaction through electrostatic attraction between Zn2+ in Zn-His-GQDs and π-electrons in the benzene ring of toluene, the π-π interaction between His-GQDs and the benzene ring of toluene [46,47], and the weak hydrogen bond formed between -NH2 in Zn-His-GQDs and -CH3 in toluene [48] were favorable to the adsorption of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene molecules.

For ethyl acetate, pore filling effect played a major role in the adsorption process. In addition, mesoporous materials can increase the collision and contact probability of ethyl acetate in Zn-His-GQDs materials, thus improving the molecule trapping ability. Meanwhile, high surface energy of His-GQDs with small size in Zn-His-GQDs can also promote the adsorption process. Thus, the adsorption capacity of Zn-His-GQDs to ethyl acetate still was high despite of its relative low specific surface area.

In order to further investigate the adsorption process of Zn-His-GQDs to VOCs simulants (toluene aqueous solution and ethyl acetate aqueous solution), the pseudo-first-order kinetic model according to Eq. (3) and the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and according to Eq. (4) were used to fit the experimental data [28,44,49].
(3)
ln
Q e
−
Q t
= ln
Q e
−
k 1
t
(4)
t
Q t
=
1
k 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
where Qe was equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg·g−1), Qe,c was fitted value of equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg·g−1), Qt was adsorption capacity at real time of t (mg·g−1), k1 was pseudo-first-order kinetic constant (min−1), and k2 was pseudo-second-order kinetic constant (g·mg−1·min−1). The kinetic fitting curves for the adsorption of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene and ethyl acetate are shown in Fig. 11 , and obtained kinetic parameters are shown in Table 4 . Correlation coefficient R2 showed that pseudo-second-order kinetic model for the adsorption of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene was preferred, and pseudo-first-order kinetic model for the adsorption of ethyl acetate was preferred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption thermodynamics of Zn-His-GQDs on toluene and ethyl acetate
ce:section.ce:para:
We carried out the adsorption thermodynamic study of toluene and ethyl acetate by Zn-His-GQDs through thermodynamic parameters including Gibbs free energy change (ΔG), enthalpy change (ΔH), and entropy change (ΔS). The thermodynamic parameters were calculated according to Eqs. (5)-(7) [50].
(5)
∆ G = − RT
InK d
(6)
K d
=
Q e
C e
(7)
InK d
=
∆ S
R
−
∆ H
RT
where ∆G is the standard Gibbs free energy change during adsorption (J·mol−1), T is the absolute temperature (K), R is the gas constant (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1), Kd is the adsorption equilibrium constant, Qe is the equilibrium adsorption amount (mg·g−1), Ce is the adsorption equilibrium concentration (mg·L−1), ∆H is the standard enthalpy change during adsorption (J·mol−1), ∆S is the standard entropy change of the adsorption process (J·mol−1·K−1).

In order to investigate the thermodynamics of Zn-His-GQDs during the adsorption of toluene and ethyl acetate, we conducted adsorption experiments at 293 K, 323 K, and 353 K. The experimental procedure was the same as that in section of 2.6. Fig. 12 demonstrates the thermodynamic fitting curves of the adsorption process of Zn-His-GQDs to toluene and ethyl acetate. The specific thermodynamic parameters are given in Tables 5 and 6
. These results showed that ΔG < 0 during the adsorption process illustrated that the adsorption of toluene and ethyl acetate by Zn-His-GQDs could be spontaneous. In addition, ΔH > 0 and ΔS > 0 for the adsorption of toluene and ethyl acetate by Zn-His-GQDs, suggested that the whole adsorption was an endothermal and entropy-increasing process.

Zn-His-GQDs were run for five adsorption-desorption cycles under the same experimental conditions (the same as Section 2.6.) to investigate the regeneration. After five cycles, the adsorption efficiency of Zn-His-GQDs on toluene solution and ethyl acetate solution was still above 59.80 % and 36.75 %, respectively (Fig. S1 in Supplementary Materials), which indicated that Zn-His-GQDs had relative high regenerative properties and could be used as the promising adsorbent for the adsorption of VOCs in industrial applications. Additionally, we investigated the stability of Zn-His-GQDs after the adsorption of toluene solution and ethyl acetate solution with different pH. These results showed that Zn-His-GQDs after the adsorption of toluene and ethyl acetate solution with different pH still possessed the framework structure similar to MOFs-like materials (Fig. S2 in Supplementary Materials) suggesting the high stability of Zn-His-GQDs during the adsorption in different pH solutions.

In this work, ZIF-8 like materials of Zn-His-GQDs were successfully prepared by using His-GQDs and zinc chloride solution. The specific surface area of as-synthesized Zn-His-GQDs was 63.3 m2 g−1 lower than ZIF-8 materials and its pore size was mainly in the range of 2–30 nm. However, mesoporous Zn-His-GQDs still exhibited excellent adsorption performances for different VOCs simulants. According to adsorption experiments, the removal rate of toluene and ethyl acetate increased under the acidic or basic conditions. The adsorption equilibrium time of Zn-His-GQDs for ethyl acetate was longer than that for toluene. When the content of Zn-His-GQDs was 200 mg, the equilibrium adsorption capacity of toluene and ethyl acetate was 350 mg·g−1 and 669.44 mg·g−1, respectively. In addition, this kind of MOFs-like materials exhibited a high regeneration and stability. Thus, this work not only provides a new route for preparing mesoporous materials, but also expands the application of ZIF-8 like materials in the removal of VOCs.

Youliang Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yue Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiaxian Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Changqing Fang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources. Jing Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiarui Zhao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization. Tong Zhao: Validation, Visualization, Methodology. Jia Zhu: Conceptualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2021YFD1600402), Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2020GXLH-Z-031), Key Scientific Research Project of Weinan City (Grant Nos. 2020ZDYF-GYCX-107 and ZDYFJH-176), Key Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 20JS106), Beilin District Science and Technology Plan Project (Grant No. GX2248), Qindu District Science and Technology Plan Project (Grant No. QK2022gy11).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110441.
